Name: Lamar Jackson
Outlook: How off-the-charts good was Lamar last year? He put together arguably the most consistent fantasy season of all-time with 16(!) top-12 finishes, the most ever for a fantasy QB. He leveled up in the passing department ranking #1 in the NFL in passer rating, yards per attempt, and fantasy points per dropback. Oh, and he ran for 915 yards. It seems he is a perfect fit for the Todd Monken offense and we can’t really bring ourselves to say anything negative about the fantasy game-breaker. We are fine pulling the trigger in the third round of your 1QB leagues or at the 1.01 in SuperFlex… he’s just that dominant for fantasy.

Name: Josh Allen
Outlook: The MVP award felt like a lifetime achievement award for Allen and fantasy managers everywhere after he registered his fifth straight top-4 fantasy season. It was perhaps his most impressive as 1st time over the last eight years a top-5 QB failed to have a top-30 WR or top-10 TE. The rushing is still an absolute cheat code and he’s now 10 rushing TDs away from tying Cam Newton’s mark for the most all-time for a QB. With the way Buffalo has shifted towards running more and more inside the red zone, it’s no secret Allen’s weekly floor and ceiling combo is the best in the business. The question for fantasy: is he worth a late 2nd/early 3rd round pick? Consider the opportunity cost of the RBs and WRs going in that range and ask yourself if you like other QB options later.

Name: Joe Burrow
Outlook: Classic Burrow… the Bengals start slow, fart around, and then Burrow goes on an absolute heater. From Week 9 on, he was the QB1 in fantasy including a stretch of 250+ passing yards and 3+ passing TDs in each. Some kid took over the Madden controls as he tied Kurt Warner (2008) for the most completions (248) ever in an 8-game span. With the team paying up for his top-2 WRs this off-season, it’s clear that this passing offense is arguably the best in the business. It also helps that the Cincy defense has a ton of holes forcing shootouts and more passing volume Burrow’s way. While we don’t love living and dying with TD rates from pocket passers, Burrow runs just enough to ignore that rule. They throw enough inside the red zone to justify his ADP.

Name: Jalen Hurts
Outlook: Add Super-bowl winning QB to the resumé of Jalen Hurts as he drop-kicked some of his naysayers and tush push haters last year. Remember all the worry about Jason Kelce retiring? Hurts had 11 TDs inside the 5-yard line (2nd only to Kyren Williams) and a whopping 51% of the team’s carries inside the 5, which is wild considering Saquon was fantasy’s RB1. Despite averaging just 18 completions and 25.5 pass attempts per game, Hurts had the most fantasy points ever for a QB with fewer than 375 pass attempts. He might carry some name fatigue for managers after basically doing the same thing every year, but he’s a locked-in top-5 QB for us.

Name: Jayden Daniels
Outlook: What more could Commanders fans ask for? He was an absolute culture changer guiding Washington to the NFC Championship game thanks to an unreal rookie season. His 75 total QB scrambles are the most of the modern era and he ranked 5th among all QBs in fantasy points per dropback. Part of his success was getting the ball out quickly (4th fastest) and accurately (3rd best adjusted completion on quick throws). Now with Deebo Samuel in the mix, he gets another playmaker. You are drafting Daniels near his ceiling but perhaps this offense ascends to another level in 2025?

Name: Bo Nix
Outlook: Nix dropkicked all his naysayers throwing for the 2nd most passing TDs (29) ever for a rookie QB. It was a slow start but he finished as the QB10 on the season and led Denver to the playoffs for the 1st time since 2015. The current ADP market expects him to maintain this level of fantasy production with the addition of Evan Engram and rookie RB RJ Harvey to bolster the offense. We expect defenses to slightly adjust to the Denver gameplan in 2025. 62% of his targets were less than 10 yards from the line of scrimmage as he sported the 4th highest check down rate in the NFL. Nevertheless, Nix’s accuracy and 25+ rushing yards per game offers a high weekly floor and a chance to repeat his fantasy success.

Name: Patrick Mahomes
Outlook: Remember when we had to make him… a streamer? Mahomes did not have a top-12 weekly finish until Week 9 and if you zoom out over his last 17 games played, Mahomes has an “F” in our Consistency Ratings. He exceeded 20+ fantasy points in just 23.5% of those contests. After posting career-lows in passing yards, YPA, and TD rate, is it time to abandon ship? Nope! The off-season talk has been centered on opening up the offense more and his ADP price tag is cheaper than it has ever been. It’s a potential opportunity to buy into a monster Mahomes campaign as long as some jabroni in your league doesn’t take him too early.

Name: Kyler Murray
Outlook: Run Kyler Run! It feels maddening for fantasy that for a player with his type of wheels we see such Jekyll & Hyde results. With only three top-5 performances in 2024, you were left begging for more, despite his highest scramble rate (6.5% of his dropbacks) since his monster QB2 season way back in 2020. He is sometimes hit-or-miss as a passer excelling in play-action and middle of the field targets, while ranking bottom-5 against the blitz. His chemistry with Marvin Harrison Jr. this year will need to improve for Kyler to hit a true fantasy ceiling. With the fantasy community moving on, we still like Kyler as a later round QB with upside to finish inside the top-5 at the end of the year if things break right… and he runs!

Name: Dak Prescott
Outlook: Another year and another opportunity to draft Dak late in your drafts. Coming off a hamstring injury which ended his season in Week 9, the fantasy community is probably suffering from name fatigue with the Dallas QB. He has been a top-12 fantasy QB in every full season played of his career, and was the QB3 overall as recently as 2023. The continuity with Brian Schottenheimer and the regression from the Dallas defense should be taken into account. His rushing dropoff is the only concern but we like Dak as a late round guy if you subscribe to Dallas’ early season schedule.

Name: Baker Mayfield
Outlook: Baker’s 2024 passing line (4,500 & 41) appears absolutely bonkers but consider this: his eight finishes inside the top-5 at the position were the most among all QBs. Yep, he was that good for fantasy including adding almost 400 yards on the ground. Despite Liam Coen’s departure, the expectations remain sky high, given the talent surrounding him is among the best in the NFL. We would be hard-pressed to believe he copies and pastes last year’s stats and the ADP price this year puts him on par with Patrick Mahomes as a “you are drafting him to start every week” type of pick.

Name: Justin Fields
Outlook: Now on his third franchise in three years, Fields once again looks like the Week 1 starter for a team that places its identity in running the ball and defense. In his six starts last year, he averaged 18.9 fantasy points and 39 rushing yards per game. Those are top-12 numbers and during his 4-year career, an astounding 43% of his fantasy points came on the ground in his starts. Fantasy football is skewed towards running QBs and in 2025, Fields is among the cheapest to invest in.

Name: Justin Herbert
Outlook: The lasting memory of 2024 Justin Herbert were the 4* INTs he threw in the Chargers playoff loss to the Texans. We used an asterisk because at least one of those was not his fault (thanks, Will Dissly!). However, if we look at the body of work over the course of the season, it was clear Herbert showed out in a couple of key areas of efficiency. He posted the highest QB rating, yards per attempt, and rush attempts of his career after the Chargers finally let him rip. His eight top-12 fantasy finishes from Week 8 on was second only to Lamar Jackson in that span. He lands in top-15 territory with a chance to hit on the right side of a TD rate variance with the weapons they added in the draft.

Name: Caleb Williams
Outlook: It gets better from here… right? All signs are pointing towards the #1 overall pick from 2024 taking a big leap forward with new head coach Ben Johnson. The front office got the message loud and clear spending on the interior of the offensive line after Williams led the NFL in sacks taken (68) and throw-aways (41). His five top-6 finishes showcased some serious weekly upside, although it’s worth mentioning that many of them came in the 2nd halves during garbage time. The upgraded weapons and offensive scheme are in place for him to be a difference-maker in 2025. The only catch is that this breakout is being baked into his ADP and counted on by almost everyone in fantasy.

Name: Jordan Love
Outlook: After his top-5 finish in 2024, it was a tale of two seasons for Jordan Love in 2025. He had an 8% TD rate through the first seven weeks of the season, but the fireworks show was soon over as that rate was cut in half. From Week 8 on he was the QB30(!) in points per game. Part of the problem is the spread out nature of the offense as the Packers were the only team in the NFL without a single receiver seeing 20+ percent of the targets. We still believe in Matt LaFleur as a play-caller and this offense being a top-10 scoring unit. If his TD rate returns more in the range of 5+ this year, Love can be an easy QB1 yet again.
